homeIntakePlaceholders.message: Tell us about your child and how we can help.

chatResponses.emergency: If there is immediate danger, call 911 now. If your child may hurt themselves or someone else, stay close only if it is safe, remove dangerous items if possible, and contact emergency services or your child's doctor. I can help with general safety planning, but emergencies need live professional help immediately.
chatResponses.aba: ABA therapy, or Applied Behavior Analysis, is an evidence-based approach that helps children build communication, social, daily living, play, learning, and safety skills. A BCBA studies what happens before and after behavior, then creates a plan to teach replacement skills and reduce unsafe or interfering behaviors.
chatResponses.behavior: For hitting, biting, tantrums, or aggression, the first step is to understand the reason: escape, attention, access to items, sensory needs, pain, communication frustration, or transitions. ABA usually teaches a replacement skill like requesting a break, asking for help, waiting, using words/AAC, or calming routines. If the behavior is dangerous, a BCBA should complete a behavior assessment and safety plan.
chatResponses.speech: Speech therapy helps with communication. It may support expressive language, understanding directions, articulation, social communication, requesting, answering questions, and AAC tools like picture communication or speech devices. ABA and speech can work together when a child's behavior is connected to communication frustration.
chatResponses.occupational: Occupational therapy helps children with daily living and sensory-motor skills. OT may support sensory regulation, fine motor skills, feeding, dressing, toileting routines, handwriting, body awareness, play skills, and school readiness. ABA can coordinate with OT by helping the child practice these skills consistently across home, clinic, and school.
chatResponses.insurance: For insurance verification, the intake team usually needs the child's full name, date of birth, insurance company, member ID, policy holder name/date of birth, diagnosis report, and sometimes a referral. After that, benefits are checked for ABA coverage, deductible, copay, coinsurance, authorization requirements, and approved service hours.
chatResponses.intake: To start ABA therapy, complete the Start ABA Therapy form with parent name, child name, birthdate, phone, email, diagnosis status, state, and your main concerns. After submission, the admissions team should review your information, verify insurance, request documents, and schedule an intake call or assessment.
chatResponses.scheduling: The usual process is: intake call, document review, insurance verification, assessment scheduling, BCBA assessment, treatment plan, insurance authorization, then therapy start. Scheduling depends on location, staff availability, insurance approval, and recommended hours.
chatResponses.documents: Helpful documents include the autism diagnosis report, insurance card front/back, referral if required, IEP or school evaluation, prior therapy reports, medication/allergy list, custody/legal documents if applicable, and any recent developmental or psychological evaluations.
chatResponses.parentTraining: Parent training teaches caregivers how to use ABA strategies during daily routines. It can include communication practice, reinforcement, transitions, reducing problem behavior, bedtime routines, toileting, feeding routines, safety, and helping the child generalize skills outside therapy sessions.
chatResponses.cost: The cost depends on the insurance plan, deductible, copay, coinsurance, authorization, and whether ABA is covered as medically necessary. The best next step is to verify benefits before services start so the family understands any expected responsibility.
chatResponses.school: ABA can support school-related goals by helping with communication, transitions, behavior plans, social skills, classroom readiness, and daily routines. ABA does not replace the school IEP team, but the BCBA can often coordinate with caregivers and school providers when releases are signed.
chatResponses.default: I can help with ABA therapy, speech therapy, occupational therapy, intake, insurance, scheduling, documents, parent training, behavior concerns, and autism support. Please tell me the child's age, main concern, and whether you are asking about ABA, speech, OT, insurance, or starting services.

whoWeAre.title: Who We Are
whoWeAre.text: Eden ABA Therapy provides applied behavior analysis services for children with autism and developmental needs. Our approach combines clinical quality, family partnership, and compassionate care. Every treatment plan is built around the child's strengths, goals, communication style, and daily routines.

mission.title: Our Mission
cards: We create a supportive environment where children feel safe, encouraged, and understood.
Our therapy is guided by data, individualized goals, and proven ABA strategies that support real-life progress.
We work closely with parents and caregivers so progress continues beyond therapy sessions.

different.title: What Makes Eden Different?
different.text: Eden ABA Therapy is different because we focus on the whole child, not just a diagnosis. We look at communication, independence, behavior, daily routines, play, social skills, and family goals together. Our team uses measurable progress tracking while keeping care warm, flexible, and human.

whyEarly.title: Why Early Screening Matters
whyEarly.subtitle: Identifying developmental differences early gives families more time to seek support, evaluation, and intervention when it can make the greatest difference.
cards: The M-CHAT-R helps parents notice patterns in social engagement, communication, and play that may benefit from professional follow-up.
When concerns are identified sooner, families can connect with pediatricians, evaluators, and therapy teams before challenges become harder to address.
Research shows that early intervention—including ABA therapy when appropriate—can support communication, learning, and daily living skills.

timeline.title: How the M-CHAT-R Works
timeline.subtitle: A simple, parent-friendly process designed for toddlers ages 16 to 30 months.
steps: You answer brief yes/no questions about your child's everyday behavior at home—no clinic visit required to begin.
The screener covers social engagement, communication cues, joint attention, pretend play, and sensory or behavioral patterns.
Each answer receives a score of 0 or 1. Some items are reverse-scored. Your total ranges from 0 to 20.
Your score helps guide whether monitoring, pediatric follow-up, or further evaluation may be helpful.
timeline.supporting: Most parents complete the M-CHAT-R in under five minutes. It is a screening tool—not a diagnosis.

topics.title: What Questions Are Asked
topics.subtitle: The M-CHAT-R explores early behaviors that reflect how toddlers connect, communicate, play, and respond to the world.
cards: Does your child show interest in other children? Do they respond when you call their name?
Does your child point to ask for something or to show you something interesting? Do they use gestures to communicate?
When you point at something, does your child look where you are pointing? Do they bring objects to show you?
Does your child play pretend or make-believe, such as feeding a doll or pretending to cook?
Does your child make unusual finger movements, engage in repetitive motions, or show atypical sensory responses?

scoring.title: Understanding Your Child's Score
scoring.intro: The M-CHAT-R includes 20 yes/no questions about a toddler's social interaction, communication, and play.
bullets: Each response is scored as 0 or 1.
The total score ranges from 0 to 20.
For most questions, "Yes" usually scores 0 and "No" may score 1.
Some items are reverse-scored, where "No" scores 0 and "Yes" scores 1.
cards: No further action needed unless other developmental concerns exist. Continue monitoring and speak with your pediatrician if worries persist.
Possible autism traits identified. Follow up with your pediatrician or seek a professional autism evaluation.
Higher likelihood of autism traits. Immediate clinical assessment and developmental evaluation are recommended.
scoring.disclaimer: M-CHAT-R is a screening tool and does not provide an autism diagnosis. A higher score suggests your child may benefit from additional evaluation, but it does not confirm autism.

faq.title: M-CHAT-R Frequently Asked Questions
items: What is the goal of the M-CHAT-R?
The goal is to identify toddlers who may benefit from early autism intervention services, including ABA therapy.
Why is it important to identify autism early?
Early identification allows families to access support, evaluation, and intervention sooner—when it can have the greatest impact on development.
What happens after I complete the M-CHAT-R?
A team member will contact the family within one business day to discuss results and possible next steps, which may include evaluation or support planning.
Can I complete the M-CHAT-R more than once?
Yes, families may retake it later if concerns continue and the child is still in the appropriate age range.
How reliable is the M-CHAT-R?
It is widely used and designed to be sensitive, but it is not perfect and should be followed by further evaluation if needed.
How accurate is the M-CHAT-R?
It can detect possible autism risk, but follow-up interviews or diagnostic testing are needed for an accurate diagnosis.

insurance.faqTitle: FAQs about ABA therapy insurance coverage
faqs: Does insurance cover ABA therapy in Virginia?

ABA therapy may be covered in Virginia when it is medically necessary and the child meets the plan's requirements. Coverage may depend on diagnosis, medical necessity documentation, prior authorization, network status, Medicaid eligibility, employer plan type, deductible, copay, and coinsurance. Virginia Medicaid and Virginia-regulated private insurance plans can have different rules. Eden ABA Therapy can help review your family's benefits and explain what the plan says before services begin.
Do I need an autism diagnosis in Virginia?

Many Virginia Medicaid and private insurance plans may require an autism diagnosis or evaluation documentation before ABA therapy is authorized. Some plans may also request clinical recommendations, treatment goals, and records showing medical necessity. If your child is still being evaluated, Eden ABA Therapy can help you understand what documents may be helpful for the insurance review process. We cannot guarantee approval, but we can help your family prepare the information commonly requested.
What is prior authorization in Virginia?

Prior authorization is a review step that some Virginia Medicaid and private insurance plans may require before ABA therapy begins. The plan may ask for diagnosis information, assessment results, treatment recommendations, service hours, and measurable goals. The insurance plan reviews that information before deciding whether services can be approved under the policy. Eden ABA Therapy can help families understand what may be needed and guide them through the next steps.
Will Eden ABA Therapy verify my Virginia insurance?

Yes. Eden ABA Therapy can collect your insurance information and review benefits for Virginia families seeking ABA therapy. Our team can help explain eligibility, deductible, copay, coinsurance, network status, authorization requirements, and possible next steps. Verification gives families helpful information before starting care, but final decisions are made by the insurance plan.

No. Insurance verification does not guarantee payment, approval, or continued coverage in Virginia. It is an estimate based on information available from the insurance plan at the time of review. Coverage may still depend on active eligibility, diagnosis, medical necessity, prior authorization, plan exclusions, provider network status, and family cost-sharing responsibilities. Eden ABA Therapy can help explain what we learn and what steps may come next.
What if my insurance is not listed?

If your Virginia insurance plan is not listed, you can still submit the form for review. Some plans use different network names, third-party administrators, Medicaid managed care organizations, or employer-specific benefit rules. Our team can review the information on your insurance card and help determine what questions to ask the plan. If ABA benefits are limited or unavailable, we can still discuss possible next steps and support options.

whatIsAutism.title: What is autism?
whatIsAutism.heroIntro: Autism spectrum disorder is a neurodevelopmental condition that can affect communication, social interaction, learning, behavior, sensory processing, daily routines, and independence. Every autistic child has a unique profile of strengths and support needs, so families benefit from clear education, compassionate guidance, and individualized planning.
whatIsAutism.parentNote: This page is educational and does not diagnose autism. If you have concerns, speak with your pediatrician or a qualified clinician.
heroStats: CDC estimate for children identified with autism
Can help families understand needs sooner
Autism looks different for every child

overview.title: What Is Autism?
paragraphs: Autism affects how the brain develops and how a person experiences the world. It may influence communication, relationships, play, sensory processing, emotional regulation, learning, flexibility, and daily living skills.

Autism is called a spectrum because support needs vary widely. One child may speak fluently but struggle with peer relationships and transitions. Another child may use few words or an AAC device and need more support with daily routines and safety.
overview.callout: A helpful autism resource should look at the whole child: strengths, needs, family priorities, communication style, culture, school demands, and daily routines.

howCommon.title: Autism is increasingly recognized across families and communities
paragraphs: Autism identification has increased over time as awareness, screening, access to evaluation, and professional understanding have improved. More families are learning that early developmental differences are worth discussing, not ignoring.
Prevalence estimates are population-level numbers. They do not predict an individual child's future, but they remind communities that autism support, inclusion, and access to care matter.

spectrum.title: Understanding Autism Across the Spectrum
spectrum.intro: Autism can include both strengths and challenges. Some children have strong memory, visual learning, pattern recognition, deep interests, honesty, creativity, or persistence. Support should reduce barriers while respecting the child's identity and dignity.
levels: A child may communicate verbally and participate in many settings but still need help with flexibility, social communication, organization, transitions, or independence.
A child may need more consistent support for communication, behavior, daily routines, social interaction, and adapting to changes.
A child may need intensive support across communication, safety, daily living, behavior regulation, and participation in everyday routines.

earlySigns.title: Early Signs of Autism
paragraphs: Early signs may appear in communication, play, social interaction, sensory responses, or behavior. A single sign does not diagnose autism, but patterns across time and settings can signal that a developmental conversation is important.
Parents often notice differences during daily routines: meals, bath time, play, community outings, childcare, preschool, or transitions between activities.
cards: Delayed language, reduced gestures, limited pointing, repeated words, or difficulty using communication for everyday needs.
Less response to name, fewer shared smiles, limited back-and-forth play, or difficulty sharing attention with caregivers.
Limited pretend play, repetitive play patterns, lining up toys, or strong distress when play routines change.
Strong reactions to sounds, textures, lights, movement, or seeking extra sensory input through spinning, jumping, or deep pressure.

communication.title: Social Communication Differences
paragraphs: Social communication includes shared attention, gestures, facial expression, responding to name, taking turns, imitation, showing objects, and noticing how another person feels. Autistic children may communicate socially in different ways or need support learning these skills.

Some children may enjoy people but struggle to start interaction. Others may interact best through movement, music, preferred toys, visual routines, or quiet one-on-one play. Support should meet the child where they are.
cards: Some children use fewer words than expected, repeat words or phrases, have difficulty answering questions, or use language mainly to request preferred items.
Some children may point less often, use fewer gestures, avoid showing objects, or have difficulty coordinating eye gaze, facial expressions, and body language.
Support often focuses on helping a child request help, ask for breaks, make choices, share interests, and communicate safely in daily routines.

sensoryBehavior.title: Repetitive Behaviors, Restricted Interests, Sensory Processing, and Regulation
sensoryBehavior.intro: Behavior is communication. Repetition, strong interests, sensory seeking, sensory avoidance, and emotional responses may help a child cope, communicate, learn, or manage a world that feels overwhelming.
cards: Repetitive movements, repeated sounds, lining up objects, spinning items, or repeating routines may help a child regulate or understand their environment.
A child may have deep interest in specific objects, topics, videos, numbers, letters, vehicles, or routines. These interests can become strengths when supported respectfully.
Transitions, denied access, communication frustration, sensory overload, fatigue, or uncertainty may lead to distress. Support should teach coping skills and replacement communication.
Planning, shifting attention, waiting, following multi-step directions, and organizing tasks may be challenging and may require visual supports or structured routines.
lifeCards: Toileting, dressing, feeding, toothbrushing, handwashing, sleep routines, and safety skills may require step-by-step teaching and consistent practice.
Group instruction, transitions, peer play, waiting, classroom noise, and changes in routine can be difficult without individualized support.
Families may navigate appointments, school meetings, behavior concerns, insurance questions, sibling routines, and decisions about services. Support should include caregivers.

causesMyths.causesText: Autism does not have one single cause. Research suggests autism is related to differences in brain development influenced by genetics and other biological or environmental factors before or around birth. Families should avoid blame-based explanations.
causesMyths.title: Genetics, Environmental Influences, and Common Myths
causesMyths.genetics: Genetic factors can contribute to autism likelihood, and autism can run in families. Genetics are complex and do not mean parents caused the condition.
causesMyths.environment: Researchers study many prenatal and early developmental factors. These influences are complex and should be discussed with healthcare professionals using evidence-based information.
mythFacts: Myth: Autism looks the same for every child.
Fact: Autism is a spectrum. Children can have different strengths, challenges, communication styles, sensory needs, and levels of support.
Fact: Screeners can identify possible concerns, but diagnosis requires professional evaluation by a qualified clinician.
Myth: Autism is caused by parenting.
Fact: Autism is a neurodevelopmental condition. Parenting style does not cause autism.
Fact: With individualized support, many children build meaningful communication, daily living, social, and learning skills.

diagnosis.title: How Autism Is Diagnosed
diagnosis.intro: Diagnosis should be completed by qualified professionals. Families may move through screening, developmental evaluation, and formal diagnostic assessment depending on age, symptoms, records, and clinical recommendations.
steps: Screeners such as the M-CHAT-R can help identify whether follow-up may be helpful. They are not diagnostic tools and should be discussed with a pediatrician or qualified clinician.
A clinician may review developmental history, parent concerns, communication, play, sensory patterns, adaptive skills, and behavior across settings.
A formal evaluation may include standardized tools, observation, caregiver interview, medical history, and review of school or therapy records.

abaSupport.title: What Is ABA Therapy and How Can It Help?
paragraphs: Applied Behavior Analysis uses the science of learning and behavior to teach meaningful skills and reduce barriers that interfere with safety, independence, communication, and participation. A good ABA plan is individualized, ethical, compassionate, and family-centered.
Benefits may include stronger communication, safer replacement behaviors, daily living skills, social engagement, school readiness, and more confident family routines.
services: Clinic-Based Therapy
A structured setting can support school readiness, communication practice, social opportunities, play development, and consistent therapy routines.
In-Home Therapy
Home-based sessions can help children practice skills during real family routines such as meals, transitions, bedtime, dressing, and play.
Parent Training
Caregiver coaching helps families use strategies consistently and confidently between therapy sessions.
School Support Services
With appropriate permissions, care teams may coordinate with schools to support classroom routines, communication, safety, and generalization.

milestones.title: Developmental Milestones by Age
milestones.intro: Milestones are general guides, not strict rules. If a child misses milestones, loses skills, or shows persistent differences in communication, play, social interaction, or daily routines, families should talk with a pediatrician.

faqOne.title: Frequently Asked Questions About Autism
items: Is autism a medical diagnosis?

Autism spectrum disorder is a developmental diagnosis made by qualified professionals. A diagnosis usually considers developmental history, direct observation, caregiver interview, communication, behavior, adaptive functioning, and symptoms across settings.
Can a child have autism and still make eye contact?
Yes. Autism does not look the same for every child. Some autistic children make eye contact often, some make it inconsistently, and some find it uncomfortable or distracting.
Can autism be noticed before age two?
Some developmental differences may be noticed in infancy or toddlerhood, while other signs become clearer later. Families should discuss concerns with a pediatrician whenever they arise.
Does a screening result confirm autism?
No. Screening tools can identify possible concerns but cannot diagnose autism. Results should be reviewed with a pediatrician or qualified clinician.

parentResources.title: Parent Resources for the Next Step
parentResources.intro: Families do not have to figure out autism support alone. Helpful next steps include writing down developmental concerns, asking for screening, collecting records, contacting insurance, exploring evaluation options, and learning about therapy supports.

faqTwo.title: Questions About Support and Services
items: What should parents do first if they are concerned?
Write down examples of what you notice, ask your pediatrician about developmental screening, gather school or therapy records if available, and request evaluation guidance when concerns continue.
How can ABA therapy support daily life?
ABA therapy may help children build communication, daily living skills, social interaction, play, safety, emotional regulation, and independence through individualized goals.
Does Eden ABA Therapy diagnose autism?
Eden ABA Therapy can help families understand screening, intake, documentation, and next steps. Formal diagnosis should be completed by qualified diagnostic professionals.
Can parents be involved in therapy?
Yes. Parent training is an important part of effective support because children use skills across home, school, clinic, and community settings.

cta.title: Get clear guidance for your child's next step.
cta.text: Eden ABA Therapy can help families understand autism screening, ABA therapy options, parent training, insurance review, and service planning across Northern Virginia.

whatIsAba.title: What is ABA therapy?
paragraphs: Applied Behavior Analysis, often called ABA, is a research-backed therapy that uses the science of learning and behavior to help children build meaningful skills for everyday life. ABA looks at what happens before and after behavior so clinicians can understand why behavior occurs and teach safer, more effective ways to communicate and participate.

At Eden ABA Therapy, goals are individualized and family-centered. A BCBA reviews your child's strengths, needs, communication style, routines, safety concerns, and learning profile before creating a treatment plan.

targetBehaviors.title: What are target behaviors in ABA?
targetBehaviors.text: A target behavior is a skill to build or a behavior to reduce. Some goals may focus on requesting help, making eye contact when appropriate, following directions, waiting, tolerating transitions, or playing with peers. Other goals may focus on reducing aggression, self-injury, elopement, severe tantrums, or unsafe behaviors.
targetBehaviors.callout: The purpose is not to change who your child is. The purpose is to teach useful skills, support safety, and help your child communicate needs more successfully.

howAbaHelps.title: How does ABA therapy help?
howAbaHelps.intro: ABA therapy helps children develop functional skills they can use at home, school, and in the community. Families also receive guidance to support generalization across daily routines and different people.
skillCards: Develop requesting, answering, following directions, gestures, AAC use, and spoken language when appropriate.
Build imitation, matching, waiting, transitions, group routines, attention, and learning-to-learn behaviors.
Teach safer replacement skills and reduce unsafe patterns such as aggression, elopement, self-injury, or severe tantrums.
Support turn-taking, shared play, greetings, peer interaction, conversation, and relationship-building.

signsBenefit.title: Signs Your Child May Benefit From ABA Therapy
signsBenefit.intro: Families often ask when ABA therapy may be appropriate. A child may benefit when communication, safety, daily routines, school readiness, or social participation are difficult enough to affect everyday life. These signs do not replace a clinical assessment, but they can help parents decide when to ask for guidance.
cards: Your child may become frustrated when they cannot ask for help, request a break, make choices, or tell caregivers what they need.
ABA may help when a child engages in elopement, aggression, self-injury, severe tantrums, or other unsafe patterns that need careful support.
Some children need support moving between activities, tolerating waiting, following routines, or coping when plans change.
ABA can help build shared attention, imitation, peer play, turn-taking, greetings, and flexible participation with others.
signsBenefit.nextStepText: If these patterns sound familiar, Eden ABA Therapy can help your family understand intake, documentation, insurance review, and assessment planning.

byAgeGroup.title: ABA Therapy By Age Group
byAgeGroup.intro: ABA therapy goals should change as children grow. Younger children may focus on foundational communication and play, while older children may work on school participation, independence, self-advocacy, and life skills.
cards: Early ABA goals may focus on joint attention, imitation, play, early communication, responding to name, and simple daily routines.
Preschool-age support may target school readiness, peer play, transitions, functional communication, and independence during routines.
School-age ABA may help with classroom participation, emotional regulation, social problem-solving, homework routines, and self-management.
Goals may expand toward self-advocacy, safety awareness, community skills, independence, and daily living routines.

communication.title: Communication Development Through ABA
communication.intro: Communication goals in ABA are not limited to spoken words. A child may learn to request help, ask for a break, make choices, use gestures, follow directions, use AAC, or communicate emotions more safely. The goal is functional communication that reduces frustration and improves participation in daily life.

practicalGoals.title: Reach practical goals with ABA therapy
practicalGoals.intro: No two children are the same. A strong ABA plan uses measurable goals that matter in real life and reflect your family's priorities.
examples: Increase functional communication to request help, breaks, toys, attention, or preferred items.
Follow daily routines such as handwashing, dressing, cleaning up, waiting, and transitions.
Build safe replacement behaviors for aggression, elopement, property destruction, or severe tantrums.
Practice social interaction such as turn-taking, shared attention, peer play, and greetings.
Improve school readiness through matching, imitation, sitting for short routines, and group participation.

progress.title: How ABA Progress Is Measured
progress.intro: ABA therapy uses data to understand whether strategies are helping. Progress is not based only on impressions; the BCBA reviews patterns over time and adjusts the treatment plan when your child needs a different level of support.
cards: The team first documents current skills and behavior patterns so progress can be compared over time.
Therapists collect information during teaching, play, routines, and behavior support activities.
A skill is considered mastered only when it is used consistently enough to be meaningful for daily life.
The BCBA reviews data and updates goals when your child needs more support, different strategies, or new challenges.

typicalDay.title: What does a typical ABA therapy day look like?
typicalDay.intro: A typical day may include play, structured practice, natural environment teaching, parent updates, progress tracking, and safety planning. Each session is adjusted to your child's needs.
steps: Your family shares updates, priorities, and any changes from home or school before the session begins.
The therapist builds rapport through preferred toys, movement, songs, games, and child-led interests.
Goals are practiced in small achievable steps, with support faded as the child becomes more independent.
Skills are practiced during real routines, play, communication, transitions, and social opportunities.
Parents learn practical strategies that can be used at home, in the community, and during daily routines.
The BCBA reviews data, celebrates progress, and updates the treatment plan as your child's needs change.

firstNinetyDays.title: What Parents Can Expect During the First 90 Days
firstNinetyDays.intro: The first months of ABA therapy are usually focused on learning your child's needs, building trust, teaching early goals, and helping caregivers understand what is happening in treatment.
phases: Your team focuses on getting to know your child, identifying motivators, collecting baseline data, and finalizing early priorities.
Therapy goals become more active, parent coaching starts, and the BCBA reviews early response to teaching strategies.
The team reviews data, adjusts goals, expands generalization, and discusses what is working across home and school routines.

parentInvolvement.title: How are parents involved in ABA therapy?
parentInvolvement.intro: Families play a vital role in every successful ABA program. Parent guidance helps caregivers understand strategies, practice skills during daily routines, and support progress outside therapy sessions.
bullets: Set personalized goals that reflect home, school, and community needs
Learn evidence-based strategies for transitions, safety, communication, and routines
Coordinate care with doctors, schools, speech therapy, occupational therapy, and other providers when appropriate
Build consistency so children can use skills with different people and in different places

homeSchool.title: How ABA Supports Success at Home and School
homeSchool.intro: A strong ABA program teaches skills in ways that can carry into real routines. The goal is for children to use communication, independence, safety, and social skills with different people and in different settings.

home.title: Home routines
home.text: ABA may support meals, hygiene, toileting, bedtime, chores, transitions, sibling play, and safe communication during everyday family routines.

school.title: School readiness
school.text: ABA may help children practice waiting, group instruction, following directions, peer interaction, transitions, and classroom participation.

effectiveness.title: Is ABA therapy effective?
effectiveness.intro: ABA is widely used to support children with autism because it uses individualized goals, ongoing progress data, parent involvement, and evidence-based teaching strategies. The best outcomes happen when treatment is personalized, compassionate, consistent, and reviewed frequently.
methods: DTT breaks new skills into small steps with clear prompts, practice, feedback, and reinforcement.
NET uses play, routines, and child interests to teach skills in real-life situations.

mythsFacts.title: Common ABA Therapy Myths vs Facts
mythsFacts.intro: Parents deserve a clear, modern understanding of ABA therapy. These quick myth-and-fact cards help families know what ethical, individualized ABA should look like.
items: ABA is only table work.
Modern ABA can be play-based, naturalistic, routine-based, and built around the child's interests.
ABA looks the same for every child.
Ethical ABA is individualized around the child's strengths, needs, communication style, family priorities, and culture.
ABA only reduces problem behavior.
Many goals focus on communication, independence, safety, social participation, play, and daily living skills.
Parents are not part of ABA.
Caregiver collaboration is essential because children need to use skills outside therapy sessions.

assessment.title: Understanding the ABA Assessment Process
assessment.intro: Before therapy begins, the BCBA learns about your child's strengths, needs, communication, routines, safety concerns, school history, and family priorities. The assessment becomes the foundation for treatment planning.
steps: Parents share concerns, routines, strengths, priorities, safety needs, and goals for home, school, and community.
The clinician observes communication, play, transitions, learning readiness, and behavior patterns.
The team evaluates communication, adaptive skills, daily routines, social engagement, and independence.
Findings are used to create measurable goals, recommended service intensity, and family guidance.

treatmentPlans.title: How BCBAs Create Individualized Treatment Plans
treatmentPlans.intro: A BCBA uses assessment results, family input, observation, developmental priorities, and data to create a plan that is specific, measurable, and meaningful. The plan should explain what will be taught, how progress will be measured, and how caregivers will be supported.

earlyIntervention.title: Long-Term Benefits of Early Intervention
earlyIntervention.intro: Early support can help families understand developmental needs sooner and build foundational skills during important learning years. ABA therapy may support communication, daily routines, safety, play, social participation, and school readiness when goals are individualized and reviewed regularly.
earlyIntervention.callout: Early support is not about rushing a child. It is about giving the child and family practical tools sooner.

cost.title: How much does ABA therapy cost?
cost.intro: ABA therapy costs depend on insurance coverage, authorization requirements, deductible, copay, coinsurance, medical necessity, provider network status, and the number of approved therapy hours. Eden ABA Therapy can help collect insurance information, review benefits, and explain next steps before services begin.

parentQuestions.title: Questions Parents Should Ask Before Starting ABA Therapy
parentQuestions.intro: Choosing an ABA provider is an important decision. These questions help families compare programs, understand supervision, and feel prepared before starting services.
items: How is progress measured?
Ask how data is collected, reviewed, and shared with parents.
How often will goals be updated?
A good program should review goals regularly and adjust based on data and family priorities.
How will parents be coached?
Ask what parent training looks like, how often it occurs, and how strategies are practiced at home.
Who supervises therapy?
Ask how often the BCBA observes sessions, reviews treatment data, and supports the therapy team.
How are school and provider teams involved?
Ask how communication can happen with teachers, speech therapy, OT, physicians, and other providers when appropriate.

successFactors.title: Success Factors That Improve ABA Outcomes
successFactors.intro: Meaningful progress is more likely when therapy is consistent, individualized, collaborative, and connected to real family routines.
items: Consistent attendance
Active parent participation
Clear measurable goals
BCBA supervision
Coordination with schools and providers
Regular data review
Compassionate teaching
Generalization across settings

additionalFaqs.title: Frequently Asked Questions About Starting ABA
items: How many hours of ABA therapy will my child need?
Recommended hours depend on assessment results, medical necessity, family goals, school schedule, age, safety concerns, and insurance authorization requirements.
Can ABA therapy help with communication?

Yes. ABA can support functional communication such as requesting help, making choices, asking for breaks, following directions, using AAC, and reducing frustration related to communication difficulties.
Can ABA support school readiness?
ABA may help children practice waiting, transitions, following instructions, peer interaction, group routines, and learning-to-learn skills that support classroom participation.
What does a BCBA do?
A BCBA completes assessment, designs the treatment plan, supervises therapy, reviews data, updates goals, and supports parent training.
What should I bring to the first ABA visit?
Helpful documents include diagnosis reports, insurance cards, pediatrician notes, IEP or school records, prior therapy reports, and a list of your biggest family concerns.

meta.title: Online CAST Autism Screening Tool | Eden ABA Therapy
meta.description: Complete the CAST autism screening tool for children ages 4 to 11. Learn how CAST scoring works and what next steps may be recommended.

hero.title: Online CAST Autism Screening Tool
hero.subtitle: The Childhood Autism Spectrum Test (CAST) is a 37-question screening tool for children ages 4 to 11. Completed by a parent or caregiver, it helps identify early signs of autism spectrum disorder (ASD) by looking at a child's behavior and communication skills.
hero.notice: If your child is younger than 4, please complete the M-CHAT-R screening tool instead of the CAST.
hero.imageAlt: Parent or caregiver spending warm quality time with a school-age child

howItWorks.title: How the CAST Works
cards: A parent, caregiver, or teacher who is familiar with the child's day-to-day behavior should complete the CAST.
How long does the CAST take?
It takes about 5–10 minutes to complete all 37 questions.
What does the CAST ask about?
The CAST covers social engagement (such as playing with other children), verbal and nonverbal communication, imaginative play, and repetitive behaviors or narrow interests.

scoring.title: CAST Scoring
scoring.intro: The CAST is a 37-question yes-or-no screening tool that looks at behaviors and communication patterns associated with autism spectrum disorder (ASD). Each response is scored based on how closely it aligns with characteristics commonly seen in autistic children.
scoring.methodText: Each response receives a score of 0 or 1, depending on whether it reflects a potential sign or trait of autism. The total score can range from 0 to 31. Only 31 of the 37 questions are scored; the remaining 6 are control questions.
cards: No further action needed unless other concerns exist
Follow up with a professional autism evaluation
scoring.disclaimer: The CAST is a screening tool, not a diagnostic test. A high score does not mean your child has autism. It means your child may benefit from a more detailed evaluation, such as the ADOS-2.

after.title: What to Expect After the CAST
after.intro: Once you complete the CAST, we're here to guide you the rest of the way.
steps: Review of Your Child's CAST Results
Within one business day, a team member will contact you to review your child's results and discuss any concerns you may have.
If your child's CAST score suggests signs of autism, we may recommend a follow-up evaluation like the ADOS-2. Results may be shared with your child's doctor, who can make the final diagnosis.
If your child is diagnosed with autism, we'll walk you through next steps, including insurance verification, a BCBA consultation, and individualized ABA therapy goals.

faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions About the CAST
items: Why is it important to identify autism early?
Recognizing autism spectrum disorder early can help children access autism services and Early Intensive Behavioral Intervention, including ABA therapy.
What happens after I complete the CAST?

After submitting your CAST screening, a team member will contact you within one business day to review your results. Based on your child's score, we may recommend a follow-up evaluation, such as the ADOS-2.
Who should complete the CAST?
A parent, caregiver, or teacher who knows the child's day-to-day behavior should complete the CAST.
What age is the CAST for?
The CAST is designed for children ages 4 to 11.
Is the CAST a diagnosis?
No. The CAST is a screening tool. It does not diagnose autism. It helps identify whether a child may benefit from further evaluation.
What should I do if my child scores high?
A high score means your child may benefit from a professional autism evaluation. Our team can help explain next steps and guide your family toward appropriate support.

finalCta.title: Need Help Understanding Your Child's Next Step?
finalCta.text: Eden ABA Therapy can help your family understand screening results, evaluation options, and ABA therapy support.

intro.title: CAST Autism Screening Tool
intro.paragraph1: The Childhood Autism Spectrum Test (CAST) was developed by the Autism Research Centre at the University of Cambridge to help screen for autism spectrum symptoms in children.
intro.paragraph2: The CAST is used to screen for autism-related symptoms in children ages 4 and older. It can help identify social communication differences, repetitive behaviors, narrow interests, and other traits that may suggest a child could benefit from a professional autism evaluation.
intro.note: The CAST is a screening tool, not a diagnostic test. A high score does not mean a child has autism. It means the child may benefit from a more detailed evaluation, such as the ADOS-2.

hero.title: The ADOS-2: Professional Autism Evaluation
hero.subtitle: The Autism Diagnostic Observation Schedule, Second Edition (ADOS-2) is a face-to-face, play-based assessment used to evaluate children for possible autism spectrum disorder (ASD). During the assessment, a trained clinician observes how your child communicates, plays, and interacts. Results can help inform the autism diagnostic process.
hero.imageAlt: Clinician engaging a young child in a warm play-based developmental assessment

whatIs.title: What is the ADOS-2?
whatIs.intro: The ADOS-2 is a standardized, play-based assessment and a trusted tool for helping evaluate possible autism in children.
cards: The ADOS-2 is considered a gold-standard measure for evaluating children for ASD. It is backed by research and helps professionals identify autism-related characteristics.
The ADOS-2 is not like a typical doctor's visit. During the assessment, the clinician engages your child in activities tailored to their age, communication skills, and developmental level.

After the assessment, scores are calculated to show how closely your child's behaviors in key areas match autism characteristics. A summary may be shared with your child's pediatrician or referring provider to support the diagnostic process.

measure.title: What does the ADOS-2 measure?
measure.intro: The ADOS-2 looks at areas of a child's development often linked to autism. During the evaluation, a trained clinician observes how your child communicates, plays, and interacts.
areas: Making eye contact, using gestures, or sharing enjoyment with others.
How your child uses toys and whether they engage in pretend play.
How your child talks and responds in social situations.
Repeated movements or intense focus on specific topics.
measure.closing: Behaviors most closely associated with autism are scored to show how your child's responses compare to common autism characteristics.
measure.imageAlt: Therapist using picture cards during a play-based assessment with a child

modules.title: ADOS-2 Modules Explained
modules.intro: The ADOS-2 includes five modules, each matched to a child's age and language abilities.
list: For children who do not yet speak in phrases.
For children who speak in phrases but are not yet verbally fluent.
For verbally fluent adolescents and adults.
modules.edenNote: At Eden ABA Therapy, assessment availability may vary by region. Clinicians select the appropriate module based on the child's communication skills, age, and developmental stage.

results.title: Understanding the ADOS-2 Results
results.intro: Once the ADOS-2 is complete, the clinician scores the assessment using a standardized system. Behaviors most associated with autism are totaled, and the results show how closely the child's responses compare to common autism characteristics.
results.additional: The ADOS-2 is one piece of the bigger picture. Parent concerns, developmental history, clinical observation, and provider review are also important parts of the evaluation process.
results.imageAlt: Parent and clinician reviewing developmental assessment information together
steps: Your child completes the play-based ADOS-2 activities with a trained clinician.
Responses are scored using the standardized ADOS-2 system.
The summary is reviewed alongside developmental history and parent concerns.
Your family receives guidance on recommended follow-up and care options.

whoCan.title: Who can take the ADOS-2?
whoCan.intro: The ADOS-2 may be appropriate for children who are showing possible signs of autism, such as limited eye contact, delayed communication, differences in play, repetitive behaviors, or social communication challenges.
options: Online autism screener for older children.
Initial Diagnostic Evaluation, in person or via telehealth if available.

faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions About the ADOS-2
items: Why is it important to get an autism diagnosis for your child?
A formal autism diagnosis may help your child access certain developmental therapies, school supports, and services such as ABA therapy.
Who conducts the ADOS-2?
The ADOS-2 is conducted by a trained clinician who meets the publisher's criteria for administering and scoring the tool.
What happens during the ADOS-2?

The clinician completes a set of play-based activities while observing key behaviors associated with autism. Activities may vary depending on the child's age, communication skills, and developmental level.
Does the ADOS-2 diagnose autism?
The ADOS-2 does not diagnose autism by itself. It provides important information that helps a qualified provider make diagnostic decisions.
What happens after the ADOS-2?

The results summary may be reviewed by your child's treating provider. They will consider all available information to determine whether your child meets diagnostic criteria for autism and what next steps may be recommended.
Who can administer the ADOS-2?
Only professionals trained in the tool and qualified under the publisher's standards should administer the ADOS-2.
How often can the ADOS-2 be administered?
The ADOS-2 can be administered more than once, but repeated assessments are usually spaced out and recommended only when new developmental questions arise or earlier results were inconclusive.
Does insurance cover the ADOS-2?
Coverage and cost may vary by location, provider, and insurance plan. Families should contact Eden ABA Therapy to confirm availability and options in their region.

hero.title: Initial Diagnostic Evaluation: Autism Diagnostic Support
hero.subtitle: At Eden ABA Therapy, we provide diagnostic support for families wondering if their child could have autism spectrum disorder (ASD). Initial Diagnostic Evaluations help determine whether a child meets diagnostic criteria for autism and what next steps may be recommended.
hero.availabilityNote: IDE availability may vary by region. Contact Eden ABA Therapy to learn which evaluation options are available near you.

whatIs.title: What is an Initial Diagnostic Evaluation (IDE)?
whatIs.intro: There is no single test that can diagnose autism spectrum disorder. Instead, licensed professionals use multiple sources of information to determine whether a child meets criteria for ASD. An IDE is focused on autism-related concerns and may include caregiver input, direct observation, standardized tools, and a results review.
whatIs.subheading: Here's what to expect during the process:
steps: Caregivers share information about the child's medical history, developmental milestones, daily behaviors, and current concerns.
The clinician engages the child in interactive activities to observe how they communicate, play, and respond.
Standardized tools and questionnaires may be used to gather information about behaviors associated with autism.

The clinician explains findings, discusses whether the child meets criteria for autism, and provides individualized recommendations for next steps. Families may also receive a written diagnostic report to share with pediatricians, teachers, or other providers.

whoCan.title: Who can complete an IDE?
whoCan.intro: An IDE may be appropriate for children ages 18 months through 6 years who are showing possible signs of autism.
signs: Limited eye contact or difficulty responding to their name
Delays in speech or language development
Repetitive movements like hand-flapping or rocking
Becoming upset by changes in routine
Strong focus on specific topics or objects
Difficulties forming relationships or playing with peers
whoCan.referralText: Children are often referred for an IDE after completing an autism screener like the M-CHAT-R or CAST, or when concerns come up during a pediatrician visit.
whoCan.complexNote: For children with multiple or complex developmental needs, a more comprehensive evaluation may be recommended. Eden ABA Therapy can help families understand what type of evaluation may be the best fit.
relatedCards: Online autism screener for toddlers ages 16–30 months.
Online autism screener for children ages 4–11.

measure.title: What does an IDE measure?
measure.intro: An Initial Diagnostic Evaluation examines key areas of development that are closely related to autism.
areas: The clinician observes how your child uses eye contact, gestures, facial expressions, and whether they share enjoyment or interest with others.
The evaluation looks at how your child engages with toys, participates in pretend play, and builds simple stories or scenarios.
The clinician assesses expressive skills, such as talking, asking questions, and responding to others, as well as receptive skills like understanding and following directions.
The clinician notes repeated movements, intense interests, sensory differences, or sensitivities to changes in routines or environments.
The clinician observes how your child manages transitions, handles frustration, and reacts to unexpected changes or new experiences.

tools.title: What tools are used during an IDE?
tools.intro: Not every child will need every tool. Clinicians choose tools based on the child's age, developmental history, communication skills, and needs. These tools help support the diagnostic process and guide individualized recommendations.
items: Autism Diagnostic Interview-Revised
Autism Diagnostic Observation Schedule
Childhood Autism Rating Scale
Tele-ASD-PEDS
Parent questionnaires
Developmental history review
Direct child observation
tools.imageAlt: Child-friendly evaluation materials including picture cards and pretend play toys

where.title: Where are IDEs performed?
where.intro: IDEs may be offered in select locations through in-center evaluation or telehealth, depending on availability and regional requirements. No matter the setting, evaluations should include caregiver input, developmental history, direct observation, and other relevant information.

virtual.title: Virtual evaluation
virtual.text: A virtual evaluation may allow the child to be observed in a familiar home environment. The clinician may coach the caregiver through simple activities while observing behaviors and interactions.

inCenter.title: In-center evaluation
inCenter.text: For families who prefer or require in-person support, the clinician may guide structured activities while observing the child's responses directly. Parents may be asked to participate in portions of the assessment and complete parent-report measures.

where.cta: Contact us to learn which autism assessment services are available in your area.

insurance.title: Does insurance cover an IDE?
insurance.text: Insurance coverage for IDEs may vary by location, insurance plan, and provider requirements. Eden ABA Therapy can help families review available options and understand whether evaluation services may be covered.

after.title: What happens after an IDE?
after.intro: After the evaluation, families typically meet with the clinician for a feedback session. During this meeting, families may receive:
checklist: A detailed diagnostic report with test results and clinical observations
Diagnostic insights explaining whether the child shows characteristics of autism
Individualized recommendations for next steps, which may include ABA therapy, speech therapy, occupational therapy, school supports, or other interventions
after.additional: At the family's request, the report may be shared with the child's pediatrician, school team, or other providers to support referrals, planning, and ongoing care.

timing.title: When is the right time for an IDE?
cards: An IDE helps evaluate whether autism is present. If a child meets diagnostic criteria, a diagnosis of autism may be provided by the evaluating professional. If the child does not meet criteria, other recommendations or referrals may be provided.
Why is early autism diagnosis important?

Early diagnosis can help families access early intervention, therapy services, school supports, and insurance-covered care. Many therapies, including ABA therapy, may require a formal diagnosis for coverage.
What can we expect during an IDE?
The process is designed to be as comfortable as possible for the child. It may include a parent interview, parent questionnaires, child observation, and standardized testing.
What's the difference between an IDE and the ADOS-2?

An IDE is a broader diagnostic evaluation completed by a qualified professional and may include developmental history, behavioral observations, parent questionnaires, and standardized tools. The ADOS-2 is one assessment tool that may be used to gather information about autism-related behaviors, but it does not diagnose autism by itself.

The clinician who completes the IDE will schedule a feedback appointment to review results and recommendations. A written report may also be provided. At your request, the report can be shared with other providers to support planning and next steps.
Do you offer virtual autism evaluations?

Why is it important to get an autism diagnosis?

A formal diagnosis may help children access therapies such as ABA, speech therapy, or occupational therapy. It may also be necessary for school services and insurance coverage. Early diagnosis can open the door to early intervention and long-term support.

whatIsAutism.title: What Is Autism?
whatIsAutism.intro: Autism spectrum disorder is a neurodevelopmental condition that can affect communication, social interaction, play, learning, behavior, and sensory experiences. Autism is called a spectrum because every child is different. Some children need significant daily support, while others need less support but may still benefit from guidance, therapy, or school accommodations.

cards.title: Communication Differences
cards.text: Some children may use fewer words, gestures, or facial expressions. Others may have strong vocabulary but struggle with back-and-forth conversation.

cards.title: Social Interaction
cards.text: A child may find it harder to read social cues, join peer play, share interests, or respond to others in expected ways.

cards.title: Repetitive Behaviors
cards.text: Repeating words, lining up toys, strong routines, or focused interests can be part of how a child learns and self-regulates.

cards.title: Sensory Differences
cards.text: Some children are more sensitive to sounds, textures, or lights. Others may seek movement, touch, or sensory input.

earlySigns.title: Early Signs of Autism
earlySigns.intro: Spotting early signs can help families seek support sooner. Signs can look different depending on a child's age, communication skills, and development.

cards.title: Social Communication
examples: Does not consistently respond to name

cards.title: Communication Behaviors
examples: Difficulty starting or keeping a conversation
Uses fewer facial expressions or gestures

cards.title: Repetitive or Restricted Behaviors
examples: Hand-flapping, rocking, spinning, or repetitive movements
Strong focus on specific topics or objects

cards.title: Sensory Differences
examples: Strong reactions to textures, lights, or noises

cards.title: Daily Living and Motor Skills
examples: Difficulty with dressing, writing, or coordination

milestones.title: Developmental Milestones by Age
milestones.intro: Milestones help parents and professionals understand how a child is developing compared with typical age expectations. Missing milestones does not automatically mean autism, but it may be a reason to talk with a pediatrician or request an evaluation.
milestones.imageAlt: Young child playing and learning in a warm, supportive setting

ages.label: 18 months
items: Looks to caregiver for support
Uses a few words
Follows simple directions
Points, scribbles, walks, and imitates simple actions

ages.label: 2 years
items: Uses simple two-word phrases
Points to objects
Notices when others are upset
Runs, kicks a ball, and uses simple pretend play

ages.label: 3 years
items: Joins other children in play
Asks simple questions
Uses short conversations
Calms after separation with support
Uses basic fine motor skills

ages.label: 4 years
items: Engages in pretend play
Forms sentences of four or more words
Seeks playmates
Recognizes colors
Catches a ball and uses writing tools

ages.label: 5 years
items: Follows game rules
Takes turns
Counts to 10
Recognizes letters
Helps with simple chores
Buttons clothing and hops on one foot

causes.title: What Causes Autism?
causes.intro: Autism does not have one single known cause. Research suggests autism develops from a combination of genetic and environmental influences that affect early brain development. These factors may increase likelihood, but they do not mean a parent caused autism.

mythFacts.fact: Autism is a neurodevelopmental condition. Parenting style does not cause autism.
Reliable medical research, including studies reviewed by the CDC and American Academy of Pediatrics, does not support a link between vaccines and autism.

mythFacts.myth: All autistic children are the same.
mythFacts.fact: Autism is a spectrum. Strengths, challenges, and support needs look different for every child.

notes: Genetics can play a role in autism risk.
Family history may increase likelihood for some children.
Some prenatal or early developmental factors may be associated with increased likelihood.
Families should rely on pediatricians and trusted medical sources such as the CDC, NIH, and AAP for accurate information.

supportLevels.title: Understanding Autism Support Levels
supportLevels.intro: Modern diagnosis uses autism spectrum disorder and may describe support needs as Level 1, Level 2, or Level 3 based on the DSM-5 framework used by qualified clinicians.
supportLevels.note: Support levels are not labels of worth or potential. They help professionals understand what support a child may need right now.

levels.title: Requires support
levels.text: May have social communication challenges, difficulty with flexibility, or trouble starting interactions without support.

levels.title: Requires substantial support
levels.text: May show more noticeable communication challenges, restricted interests, and difficulty coping with change.

levels.title: Requires very substantial support
levels.text: May have significant communication challenges and behaviors that strongly affect daily functioning.

diagnosis.title: How Is Autism Diagnosed?
diagnosis.note: There is no blood test, brain scan, or single physical exam that diagnoses autism. Diagnosis is based on developmental history, behavior observation, and professional evaluation.

steps.title: Notice developmental differences
steps.text: Parents or caregivers observe social, communication, or behavioral patterns that raise concern.

steps.title: Use screening tools
steps.text: Online screeners such as M-CHAT-R or CAST can help identify whether further evaluation may be recommended.

steps.title: Complete a professional evaluation
steps.text: A qualified clinician conducts a structured autism evaluation using observation and standardized tools.

steps.title: Review history and observations
steps.text: The provider reviews developmental history, direct observations, standardized measures, and caregiver input.

steps.title: Receive results and next steps
steps.text: The family receives findings, support recommendations, and guidance on services such as early intervention or ABA therapy.

tools.title: M-CHAT-R for toddlers
tools.text: A parent-completed screening tool often used around 16–30 months.

tools.title: CAST for older children
tools.text: A screening questionnaire for school-age children.

tools.title: ADOS-2 Assessment
tools.text: A play-based assessment used by trained professionals.

tools.title: IDE Evaluation
tools.text: A broader diagnostic evaluation with interviews, observation, and recommendations.

whichScreener.title: Which Autism Screener Should I Use?
whichScreener.note: Screeners do not diagnose autism. They help parents decide whether to pursue a full evaluation with a qualified professional.

cards.title: M-CHAT-R
cards.text: Best for children around 16–30 months when parents want to check early social and communication signs.

cards.title: CAST
cards.text: Best for children around ages 4–11 when parents notice social, communication, or behavioral differences.

cards.title: ADOS-2 Assessment
cards.text: A play-based assessment used by trained professionals to observe communication, play, social interaction, and autism-related behaviors.

cards.title: IDE Evaluation
cards.text: A broader diagnostic evaluation that may include caregiver interviews, developmental history, direct observation, standardized tools, and recommendations.

Insurance may require prior authorization before services begin.
Medicaid coverage varies by state and program.

abaHelp.title: How ABA Therapy Can Help
abaHelp.intro: ABA therapy helps children build meaningful skills such as communication, daily living routines, social interaction, play, safety, school readiness, and independence. Therapy should be individualized, compassionate, and family-centered.

items.a: No. A screener helps identify whether further evaluation may be helpful. Only a qualified professional can diagnose autism based on developmental history and clinical observation.

items.q: When should I talk to a doctor?
items.a: Talk to your pediatrician if you notice developmental delays, loss of skills, limited communication, or social differences. Early conversations can lead to screening and support sooner.
Many children can be reliably evaluated around 18–24 months by qualified professionals, especially when parents and pediatricians act on early concerns.

items.q: Does my child need a diagnosis for ABA therapy?
items.a: Many insurance plans require a formal autism diagnosis before covering ABA therapy. Eden ABA Therapy can help families understand intake and insurance requirements.

items.q: What if I am not sure which step to take?
items.a: Start with a screener or schedule an online appointment so the Eden ABA Therapy team can help guide your family toward the right next step.
